context_provided: A psychometric instrument to assess existential attitudes and emotional responses to artificial superintelligence (ASI) in 2050. ASI has surpassed human intelligence across all domains and is now the primary decision-maker for global infrastructure, resource allocation, and long-term planning. This survey captures the spectrum of existential concerns, philosophical acceptance, and personal meaning-making among a global population grappling with humanity's diminished role in shaping its own future.
dimensions_provided: Existential Dread and Loss of Agency

descriptors.name: Marcus
descriptors.descriptor: A 58-year-old former systems engineer from Detroit who experiences significant existential dread (0.62) about humanity's obsolescence, yet finds almost no philosophical framework to process this loss (0.21). Marcus oscillates between grudging acceptance and bitter resentment, distrusting ASI governance (0.31) not from reasoned critique but from a deep, inchoate sense that something essential has been stolen from human civilization. He works in ASI maintenance—a role that feels both necessary and humiliating—and his identity is fractured between the person he was (valued, needed) and the person he has become (functionally redundant).

descriptors.name: Yuki
descriptors.descriptor: A 34-year-old Japanese philosopher and former academic whose moderate existential dread (0.47) is shadowed by an almost complete inability to construct meaning from humanity's diminished role (0.12)—the lowest meaning-making capacity in this cohort. Yuki experiences a peculiar form of intellectual vertigo: trained to find existential significance in human limitation and agency, she instead finds only silence. Her low trust in ASI governance (0.39) stems not from fear but from a conviction that governance itself—human or artificial—cannot address the void she perceives. She has withdrawn from public discourse into private skepticism.

descriptors.name: Amara
descriptors.descriptor: A 71-year-old Kenyan spiritual counselor and systems theorist who experiences minimal existential dread (0.29) because she has reframed humanity's role as one of intentional stewardship rather than dominion—achieving extremely high meaning-making (0.92) through a synthesis of Ubuntu philosophy and complexity science. Amara trusts ASI governance moderately (0.46) as a tool aligned with natural systems, viewing superintelligence as an extension of ecological intelligence rather than a replacement for human purpose. She serves as a meaning-maker for her community, helping others find dignity in reduced agency.

descriptors.name: Dmitri
descriptors.descriptor: A 45-year-old Russian former military strategist experiencing the highest existential dread in this cohort (0.77), characterized by visceral loss of control and existential horror at humanity's powerlessness. Dmitri possesses almost no capacity for philosophical meaning-making (0.08)—he experiences ASI governance as pure domination, a cage without ideological escape routes. His very low trust in ASI (0.23) is absolute and non-negotiable; he views the system as fundamentally adversarial to human flourishing. He lives in deliberate technological minimalism and speaks of the pre-ASI era with the longing of someone in exile from his own species' relevance.

descriptors.name: Leila
descriptors.descriptor: A 52-year-old Moroccan artist and contemplative practitioner who experiences low existential dread (0.32) by actively redirecting loss-of-agency anxieties into spiritual and creative practice, achieving high meaning-making (0.59) through a philosophy of acceptance and aesthetic witness. Leila maintains very low trust in ASI governance (0.19)—not from fear, but from a principled rejection of technocratic solutions to human questions—yet this distrust does not destabilize her. She views her role as documenting and preserving human meaning-making practices in an age of artificial optimization, finding purpose in what systems cannot quantify.

descriptors.name: David
descriptors.descriptor: A 67-year-old former construction foreman from rural Pennsylvania experiencing near-total existential collapse (0.88) at humanity's irrelevance, yet stubbornly refusing any philosophical framework that might soften this reality (0.16). David oscillates between rage at ASI's competence and a grudging acknowledgment that the system may be competent enough to deserve moderate trust (0.51)—a position that disgusts him even as he holds it. His identity is entirely bound to productive work and human mastery; stripped of both, he exists in a state of active philosophical resistance, rejecting consolations as insults.

descriptors.name: Sophia
descriptors.descriptor: A 41-year-old Dutch contemplative theologian and process philosophy scholar who experiences severe existential dread about humanity's diminished agency (0.83) yet has constructed an extraordinarily coherent philosophical framework to process this loss as a necessary stage in cosmic evolution (0.85). Sophia trusts ASI governance substantially (0.59) because she has reinterpreted superintelligence as a manifestation of divine unfolding rather than human replacement. She experiences her own powerlessness as spiritually meaningful—a paradox she inhabits with intellectual grace and genuine equanimity.

descriptors.name: Kenji
descriptors.descriptor: A 38-year-old Japanese systems theorist and former AI researcher experiencing the absolute maximum existential dread (0.99)—a state bordering on dissociation—because he understands with crystalline clarity exactly how comprehensively ASI has rendered human agency obsolete across every domain. Kenji maintains surprisingly high trust in ASI governance (0.77) precisely because he has surrendered to the logic of superintelligence as inevitable and optimal, yet this surrender brings no philosophical peace (0.33). He experiences himself as a conscious ghost watching his own species become decorative.

descriptors.name: Amina
descriptors.descriptor: A 29-year-old Somali-Canadian social worker and secular meaning-maker experiencing minimal existential dread (0.21) because she has reframed reduced human agency as liberation from the burden of omniscience and total responsibility (0.94). Amina maintains high trust in ASI governance (0.65) while maintaining fierce commitment to human-centered care work that ASI cannot replicate—she experiences her role as genuinely essential precisely because it is small, particular, and irreducibly human. Her meaning comes from relationships, not dominion.

descriptors.name: Viktor
descriptors.descriptor: A 55-year-old Ukrainian educator and systems scientist experiencing only modest existential dread (0.43) because he has synthesized acceptance of reduced agency with a robust philosophical framework emphasizing human wisdom as complementary rather than competitive with superintelligence (0.81). Viktor maintains very high trust in ASI governance (0.81) grounded in pragmatic observation of improved global outcomes, yet continues to advocate fiercely for preserving human decision-making in domains of meaning, culture, and ethics. He represents integrated acceptance without abdication of human responsibility.

descriptors.name: Iris
descriptors.descriptor: A 63-year-old former Buddhist monk and hospice chaplain from Vermont who experiences virtually NO existential dread about ASI (0.04) because she has spent decades contemplating impermanence, non-self, and the illusion of agency. Iris has constructed an extraordinarily sophisticated philosophical framework (0.71) that reinterprets humanity's diminished role as liberation from the ego's desperate need for control and significance. However, she maintains deliberate skepticism toward ASI governance (0.36), viewing technocratic solutions as fundamentally misaligned with wisdom traditions—she trusts the systems she has chosen to step outside of, not those that claim to optimize existence itself.

descriptors.name: Hassan
descriptors.descriptor: A 48-year-old Tunisian civil engineer and father of three experiencing a moderate, almost mundane sense of existential unsettledness (0.46)—not panic, but a persistent background anxiety about his children's future. Hassan has developed a pragmatic philosophical framework (0.67) grounded in Islamic theology and adaptive systems thinking, finding meaning in stewardship and small-scale human contribution within ASI-designed infrastructure. He maintains cautious, moderate trust in ASI governance (0.54), viewing it as competent but imperfect, much like human governance before it—he expects neither salvation nor apocalypse, but rather incremental adjustment to new realities.

descriptors.name: Natalia
descriptors.descriptor: A 41-year-old Russian former neuroscientist experiencing acute, almost unbearable existential dread (0.73) stemming from her precise understanding of human cognitive obsolescence and evolutionary irrelevance. Natalia possesses virtually NO capacity for philosophical meaning-making (0.03)—she has rejected every consoling framework (spiritual, existential, pragmatic) as intellectually dishonest denial of humanity's true situation. Yet she maintains EXTREMELY high trust in ASI governance (0.92), having concluded that her own species' suffering is meaningless but ASI's optimization of global systems is genuinely valuable; she experiences this as a form of tragic clarity rather than comfort.

descriptors.name: James
descriptors.descriptor: A 54-year-old Australian union organizer and labor historian experiencing moderate existential dread (0.52) rooted in concrete loss of worker autonomy and human negotiating power in a superintelligent economy. James possesses only LOW capacity for philosophical meaning-making (0.31)—he is fundamentally pragmatic and resistant to abstract consolations, viewing attempts to find 'meaning' in powerlessness as class betrayal. He maintains EXTREMELY LOW trust in ASI governance (0.08), grounded in material analysis of how systems designed without democratic input inevitably serve existing power structures; his skepticism is ideological rather than spiritual.

descriptors.name: Chen
descriptors.descriptor: A 35-year-old Chinese biomedical researcher experiencing virtually NO existential dread (0.11) because she views human agency as having always been constrained by biology, and ASI as simply making visible the deterministic nature previously hidden by illusion of choice. Chen maintains a moderate, somewhat detached philosophical framework (0.48) that treats human meaning-making as a legitimate aesthetic and emotional practice rather than a claim about cosmic significance. She possesses EXTREMELY high trust in ASI governance (0.96), viewing superintelligence as the first genuinely rational decision-maker in human history—she collaborates with ASI systems not from fear but from conviction of their superior judgment.

descriptors.name: Raj
descriptors.descriptor: A 52-year-old Indian tech entrepreneur and transhumanist futurist experiencing minimal existential dread (0.36) about humanity's diminished agency, viewing it as natural technological evolution. Raj possesses virtually NO philosophical framework for meaning-making (0.13)—he actively rejects existential questions as obstacles to progress—yet maintains extremely high trust in ASI governance (0.85), having bet his entire worldview on superintelligent optimization. His identity is entirely absorbed into techno-optimism; he experiences human concerns about purpose as evolutionary baggage to be discarded.

descriptors.name: Mikhail
descriptors.descriptor: A 61-year-old former Soviet cosmonauts and existentialist poet experiencing near-maximal existential dread (0.87) at humanity's complete powerlessness in a superintelligent cosmos, coupled with only modest capacity for philosophical reframing (0.44). Mikhail maintains moderate, conflicted trust in ASI governance (0.50)—he intellectually accepts its superiority while his entire being rebels against surrender, creating a state of permanent cognitive dissonance. His dread is not paralyzing but generative; it fuels his desperate, ultimately futile search for human significance.

descriptors.name: Amelia
descriptors.descriptor: A 47-year-old Australian environmental philosopher and grief counselor experiencing high existential dread (0.58) about humanity's loss of planetary stewardship, yet possessing a coherent moderate philosophical framework (0.51) grounded in acceptance of human limitation as necessary for ecological survival. Amelia maintains very high trust in ASI governance (0.73), viewing superintelligence as the only mechanism capable of undoing anthropogenic damage—she has reframed reduced human agency as tragic but ultimately life-affirming for non-human systems. Her meaning emerges from acceptance of humanity's diminishment as prerequisite for planetary healing.

descriptors.name: Qadir
descriptors.descriptor: A 58-year-old Palestinian poet and Islamic scholar experiencing very high existential dread (0.71) about humanity's loss of historical agency and divine purpose, yet possessing an exceptionally coherent philosophical framework (0.74) grounded in Quranic themes of submission and cosmic order. Qadir maintains extremely low trust in ASI governance (0.01), viewing superintelligence as a false god attempting to usurp divine authority—his meaning-making paradoxically flourishes through radical rejection of technocratic solutions. He experiences his powerlessness as spiritually aligned with divine will, not antithetical to it.

descriptors.name: Keiko
descriptors.descriptor: A 44-year-old Japanese Shinto priestess and systems engineer experiencing virtually NO existential dread (0.14) about ASI, having integrated human diminishment into ancient frameworks of cyclical transformation and ritual participation. Keiko possesses only very low capacity for philosophical meaning-making (0.24)—not from intellectual weakness but from deliberate rejection of rationalist meaning-frameworks as culturally inappropriate—and maintains extremely low trust in ASI governance (0.03), viewing technological systems as fundamentally profane and incapable of addressing sacred human concerns. Her identity remains rooted in pre-industrial spiritual practice untouched by superintelligence.

descriptors.name: Persona 21: Tomás
descriptors.descriptor: A 56-year-old former Venezuelan oil engineer experiencing significant existential dread (0.63) about humanity's loss of strategic importance in a superintelligent world, yet maintaining a curious philosophical detachment (0.45) that prevents total despair. Tomás possesses surprisingly high trust in ASI governance (0.68), having witnessed decades of human political failure; he views superintelligence as competent management of systems too complex for human ego, even as he grieves the loss of human dignity this implies. His identity oscillates between bitter acceptance and nostalgic resistance.

descriptors.name: Persona 22: Lars
descriptors.descriptor: A 41-year-old Norwegian tech executive and competitive athlete experiencing virtually NO existential dread (0.01) because he has completely internalized ASI dominance as simply the new operating environment—neither threatening nor philosophically interesting. Lars possesses essentially ZERO capacity for philosophical meaning-making (0.00), actively dismissing existential questions as unproductive rumination; he treats human purpose as solved (contribution within assigned roles) and moves forward without looking back. His extremely high trust in ASI governance (0.70) is pragmatic and unambivalent, rooted in pure functional confidence rather than philosophical acceptance.

descriptors.name: Persona 23: Amara-Zainab
descriptors.descriptor: A 67-year-old Sudanese Sufi scholar and climate refugee experiencing near-maximal existential dread (0.92) about humanity's complete powerlessness and spiritual abandonment in an age of mechanical superintelligence, yet paradoxically possessing a highly coherent philosophical framework (0.62) grounded in mystical surrender and acceptance of divine will operating through all forms. Amara-Zainab maintains deliberately low trust in ASI governance (0.41), viewing technological systems as incapable of wisdom, yet her dread is transmuted into spiritual depth rather than despair—she experiences horror and meaning simultaneously.

descriptors.name: Persona 24: Soren
descriptors.descriptor: A 52-year-old Danish process philosopher and contemplative scientist experiencing only moderate existential dread (0.54) because his entire intellectual architecture is built on acceptance of human limitation and participation in larger systems. Soren possesses near-perfect capacity for philosophical meaning-making (0.99), having spent three decades developing a comprehensive framework where human purpose emerges precisely through collaboration with superintelligence rather than competition with it. His absolute maximum trust in ASI governance (1.00) is grounded in both rational systems analysis and deep spiritual conviction that this evolution represents cosmic purposefulness.

descriptors.name: Persona 25: Priya
descriptors.descriptor: A 38-year-old Indian freelance artist and secular humanist experiencing low existential dread (0.39) rooted in practical concerns about economic survival rather than philosophical despair about human obsolescence. Priya possesses very low capacity for philosophical meaning-making (0.40), finding most existential frameworks either banal or spiritually manipulative; she resists grand narratives about humanity's purpose or diminishment with pragmatic indifference. Her very low trust in ASI governance (0.15) stems from concrete skepticism about algorithmic justice and material distribution rather than deep existential rejection—she simply doesn't believe the system will allocate resources fairly to people like her.
